speaker: interviewer
text: April 18th, 1995. We're interviewing Dolf Ringel, D-O-L-F, last name, R-I-N-G-E-L. I'm Bonnie Dwork, D as in David, W-O-R-K. We are in New York, USA. And we are speaking English. Bonnie Dwork interviewing Dolf Ringel in New York on April the 18th, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Dolf Ringel. I live in Kew Gardens, New York since 1949 when I came here with Dottie from Amsterdam, Holland. I was born in Western Germany in the city called Mainz on the 23rd of December, 1910, the third child and first son of Max and Toni Ringel. My parents at that time had a business of importing eggs from different parts of Europe, mainly Holland and Belgium. During World War One, my mother and her four children-- my brother was born in 1913-- left Mainz and went to Berlin to escape the first bombardments of Mainz during World War One. After the war-- I think it was 1922 or 1923-- my father and mother returned with the four children to Frankfurt, which was a much larger and more metropolitan city than Mainz. He continued doing the same business as before, importing eggs from mainly Western Europe, but also Eastern Europe. The business was flourishing. And my two sisters got married, in 1925 my eldest sister, and in 1929 my younger sister, both in Berlin. My brother and I went to a what is called gymnasium in Frankfurt, meaning that it taught the Latin and was a preparatory school for college. I was a very good student. And when I made my abiturium-- that means the high school-- when I got my high school diploma-- I decided to go study medicine. And I went to the medical college in Frankfurt, of the Frankfurt University. I had-- I made my, what is called physical, or translated, it may mean pre-clinical exam. But when I reached the time to start with my clinical studies, it was March 1933 and Hitler had just come to power. My father-- my father's business was not going well. Because of the dire economic situation in Germany, imports had been curtailed, and my father's business almost stopped. His business relationships and friends in Holland and Belgium informed him that Spain had become one of the largest importers of eggs in Western Europe. And they suggested that he make a business trip to Spain to find out whether there was any chance to work together in Spain. Barcelona they recommended as the commercial city where it would be the best place to-- to go to. Since my father didn't speak French, but I did, and I had my spring vacation in college, he said to me to come along to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: To Spain?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Spain. To accompanying him to Spain. We went--

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke Spanish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope. Neither one of us spoke one word of Spanish. We went first to Holland, to Belgium, spoke to our friends, and we continued to Spain. This is then March 1933. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Shortly after-- after-- a few weeks after our arrival, a letter came from my brother from Frankfurt-- he had stayed behind with my mother-- to informing us that one of the first anti-Jewish decrees of the new Nazi regime was to ban all Jews from college and also Jewish professors. In other words, my medical career in Germany had ended. So did my brother's, who had also started a medical career in Holland-- in Germany. Right then and there, I-- my father and I decided, we are not going back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you experienced any anti-Semitism prior to leaving for Spain, before 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, of course, seen the growing Nazi danger in Germany by the constant marches of the brown shirts through the streets of Frankfurt, and clashes between the Nazis and the Socialists and especially the Communists. And I worried about the political condition in Germany. My father, from-- from the early earliest beginnings of Nazism, I remember that he always mentioned, these people are dangerous. These-- I consider them murderers and we should never fall into their hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally, now and then, I did. But in-- obviously, in the universities, for instance, even before Hitler came to power, Jews were not admitted to the fraternities, because they were, by and large, anti-Semitic. And-- nor did I aspire to become a member of those fraternities. Yes, on many occasions, I heard anti-Semitic remarks. And I had also noticed that neighbors, Gentile neighbors, young boys, lived in the same building as I did, once they were in a uniform, in 1932 approximately, they never spoke to us nor-- nor acknowledged my greetings or didn't-- didn't know us anymore. We were now two different races. They were Aryans and we were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankfurt had one of the oldest Jewish community, dating back perhaps 800 years, communities that started to develop from Caesar, the Roman times, when Jews with the Roman army came down along the Rhine and established themselves along [BACKROUND NOISE] that-- that main route of commerce.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was more interested in your particular participation in the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yes. I was a member of the great Jewish community in Frankfurt. I mention great Jewish community because that was the majority and was not Orthodox. They were what we would call here Conservative, leaning towards Orthodoxism. The school that I went to, the Philanthropin, was a Jewish school founded by the Rothschild-- famous Rothschild family 100 years before. And so I was never exposed to the attacks and -and vilification that Jewish children who went to regular schools-- to-- to Gentile schools were. So I was sheltered from-- from all the developing racial strife and attacks. My father was an Orthodox Jew. And my brother and I and my father went to the synagogue every Saturday and all the holidays. So I-- my upbringing was Orthodox, following in the footsteps of my father, but not to the degree that he was brought up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So now when we arrived in Spain, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- after a few weeks, always speaking French in in Barcelona, which many people understood, because of the-- of the Catalan, a dialect spoken in Catalunya, a province bordering on France, which made it easier for me, and of course, easier for my father to start establishing business relations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A month, or maybe two month after I had arrived in Spain, I went to see the dean of the University of Barcelona, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to find out whether I could continue my studies of medicine at the University of Barcelona. To my deep regret, I was told that that was impossible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Western European countries, including Germany, never recognized diplomas, not even high school diplomas, of-- obtained in Spain, because Spain culturally and as far as education was concerned was by far not up to the level of other Western or middle-- middle European countries. Therefore, Spain retaliated by not recognizing any diplomas or high school diplomas obtained in those countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the dean told me, if you want to study medicine here, your diplomas are worth nothing. And the first thing to do is you will have to go to-- to high school. You will have to learn Spanish. And you will have to earn your high school diploma again by when you master the language. Then, when you have your-- your-- your high school degree, you-- you can try again to be admitted to school here. But, my dear boy, you will have to start from scratch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Since my father, as I mentioned before, did not speak French, and neither one of us spoke Spanish, there was only one possibility to earn a living. And that was that I took over and advised my father, and not only advise him, but I was the one who started to build up a new business once we had decided not to go back. By the summer of that same year, 1933, my brother, my mother, and both married sisters with their families came to Spain, because the-- the anti-Semitic decrees started making life already difficult for Jews in Germany, in-- as far as earning a living was concerned. So at least we-- our family was together in Barcelona. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It took me approximately six month to learn Spanish. And I learned it rather fluently, very much helped by my knowledge of Latin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So did my brother, he learned the-- the language very quickly. And we both became the-- the earners of a living, since my father was only able to advise us, and-- because of his experience. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the summer of 1936, the-- the Spanish Civil War broke out. From that time on, import of eggs was no longer possible. And that forced us to start butter business. The republic of Spain had no foreign currency, so imports were only allowed if, in turn, you could export Spanish products, and then use that foreign currency, that money, to import essential and necessary products from those countries. And that's exactly what we did. We exported hazelnuts and almonds and Spanish wine to Holland and Belgium, and imported Dutch and Belgian eggs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1937, my father started ailing. He had asthma and he had a stomach ailment. And we, the family, thought the time had come that my parents leave war-torn Spain. Medicine was not easily available anymore. The hospitals were poor. There were not enough doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism in Spain?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever. They had for-- for close to 500 years seen no Jews anymore, so they didn't even know what Jews were. So there was no anti-Semitism. We still had the German passports. And traveling to any country in the world had become extremely difficult for-- for Jews. Nobody wanted them. But Holland was the only Western-- Western European country that did not require that a visa-- a visa for Germans. So that's why we went to Holland with my father. I went with my parents and established them there, find a-- found a rented-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not apartment, but a couple of rooms where they could stay, and I returned to Spain. But six month later, it was the beginning of 1938 already, I got a telegram from my mother that my father was in serious condition in hospital and she needed help. So I left Spain, which was difficult, very difficult in those days already, and returned to Holland, and stayed there, together with my mother until my father recuperated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to-- I set up an office in Amsterdam, after I had rented an apartment for my parents, and tried to do business with my brother, the business I mentioned before, which we could do until the-- the summer of 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Civil War had ended in Spain. My brother had no desire to stay in that Franco dictatorial country any longer, was able to get a visa for London, where he had been before for business, and I met him in London, after he arrived there. That was August, 1939. But it was almost certain at that time already that war would break out in Europe. It was already after Munich, the infamous Munich Agreement. Czechoslovakia had already been absorbed into-- into Nazi Germany. And there-- there were threatening rumors and-- and newspaper reports about Hitler preparing an attack on Poland. I said goodbye to my brother, and returned to Amsterdam, because I didn't want my parents to stay alone in Holland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, in fall 1939, Holland was still neutral. The-- the war had already started. A friend of mine, who had been in the same school as I in Frankfurt, persuaded me to become a member of a sports club, Maccabi, in Amsterdam. And around Hanukkah time, which was early December 1939, he took me along to the club, introduced me there. And I became a member and started playing ping pong, when I noticed a very beautiful girl walking around there, selling some kind of candy, chocolate bars, et cetera, as a volunteer for a Jewish Zionist group in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I fell in love with her at first sight. And I-- my playing ping pong wasn't too good anymore. And I followed her with my eyes. And when I saw that she started-- prepared to leave, I quickly said to my friend, I'm leaving, and got my-- my bicycle, and approached her and introduced myself, and said, where do you live? It so happened that she lived only about 50 yards from where we lived. So I-- I ask her whether I could accompany her, which I believe reluctantly she accepted. Well, on-- on the way home, which took about 10 minutes or so, 15 minutes walk perhaps, I tried to impress her by telling her of my experiences in Spain and the languages and the travels that I had done, and all kind of stuff that didn't interest her in the least. So when I finally said goodnight to her and left, I thought, that was it. I'll never see her again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Shortly afterwards, maybe a couple of weeks later, I got a telephone call from my same friend. And he said, we are planning to have a New Year's Eve party, only we don't know where. He said, I noticed you were quite interested in [INAUDIBLE] Parfumier.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, how do you know? He said, an idiot could have noticed it. Would you be willing to have the party in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told my mother. And she said, by all means. That's when I had the chance to call her and invite her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That brought us a little closer, but still no friendship yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, 1940 came. And in April, I got a call again from my friend or he told me, he said, Maccabi, is now starting the tennis, to prepare the tennis season. Since you are member, why don't you-- why don't you join to tennis club? And I did when he said that [INAUDIBLE] Parfumier is for years already a member there and is a very good tennis player. I also played tennis, but not as well as she did.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I met then my future wife. We became friendly and started dating. On May 10, 1940, the Germans invaded Holland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to mention that from the day that the war broke out, 1st September-- 3rd of September 1939, until that time, I tried desperately to obtain any kind of visa. The American visa was out of the question. They told us we would have to wait maybe six years before-- before our quota number would be reached.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tried to-- I went to South American consul, since I spoke fluent Spanish. And I was inexperienced. I didn't understand at that time that you had to bribe them. You had to give money to a consul of Chile or to Paraguay or to Peru or wherever, these small Latin American countries, to get a visa. I didn't know it. And that's why I didn't succeed-- fortunately, didn't succeed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the war was on. The year 1940 was rather quiet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Apparently, the Germans, unlike in countries in Eastern Europe, didn't want to show the Western-- to the Western European countries, especially in Holland, which they considered an Aryan country, that they were using inhuman and brutal, murderous methods against Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nothing much happened in 1940. But '41 brought a big change. The first anti-Jewish decrees came off, and from then on, one after the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: It started out with creating a ghetto in Amsterdam, the main Jewish city in Holland. The ghetto was the part where Rembrandt had lived, a 500-year-old downtown area of Amsterdam, where mainly Jewish and Jewish businesses and stores were. A ghetto was erected there. And anyone who entered-- Gentiles were forced to leave that area and Jews who entered that area had to show an identity card. These identity cards were-- had been distributed in 1940 already. And any Jewish holder of that identity card got a-- a stamp, a big, black J. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These identity cards entitled the holder to obtain a distribution-- food distribution card. And we had to-- a little coupons. And for each food-- item of food or textile that you purchased, coupons had to be put in. Once you had exhausted your coupons, you had to wait until the next month that you got new coupons. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Like most Western European countries, Holland had a system of central register offices. That means that every child, every inhabitant of a city or town or village, had to be registered in this central office in a-- in a system that showed the card, date of birth, address, and religion. And that fact, that the religion was shown on these cards, on this registry, contributed to the catastrophe that befell the Jews in Holland and in other European countries, because when the Germans invaded Holland, one of the first things they did was to occupy immediately the, what is called in Holland, the [DUTCH], in German, in Germany, [GERMAN], and take a hold of all these data, which enabled them to later get the Jews together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1941 started, as I said, with a lot of new decrees.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll then continue, OK?
